Virtual Training: Unlocking TitanInsights
description:
Unlocking TitanInsights Presented by ASFR

Discover how TitanInsights transforms raw finance data into clear, actionable insights. In this session, you’ll learn the platform’s core features and see a live demo of CSUF’s finance data warehouse, giving you the confidence to navigate and leverage campus financial data with ease.

Target Audience: Campus Wide

Location: Join Via Zoom

No registration required. This session will be recorded and posted on our CSUF Financial Services YouTube Channel.

title: Brightwork Quartet | Ring of Fire: Music from Around the Pacific
description:
Brightwork Quartet | Ring of Fire: Music from Around the Pacific

Wednesday, March 4 at 8pm
Meng Concert Hall



Brightwork Quartet: Aron Kallay (piano), Shalini Vijayan (violin), Maggie Parkins (cello), and Brian Walsh (clarinet)

Chinary Ung | Spiral VI 
Gabriela Lena Frank | Hilos 
Pamela Madsen | Starry Adventure
Andy Akiho | Lost on Chiaroscuro Street 

 ABOUT THE PROGRAM

California sits on the eastern edge of the Pacific’s "Ring of Fire, “a vast circle of volcanoes and seismic energy encompassing nearly 75% of the planet’s active volcanoes. Brightwork embarks on a musical voyage across this ring—from Peru to Cambodia—revealing the interconnected voices and rhythms of cultures that share this dynamic boundary.

title: Once Upon a Mattress
description:
Once Upon a Mattress

Music by Mary Rogers
Lyrics by Marshall Barer
Book by Jay Thompson, Dean Fuller, and Marshall Barer
Directed by Josh Grisetti

Little Theatre

March 5 – 14, 2026

8pm: 3/5, 3/6, 3/7, 3/11, 3/12, 3/13, 3/14

2pm: 3/7, 3/14

“Once Upon a Mattress” is an uproarious update of Hans Christian Andersen’s “Princess and the Pea” set in a far-off land where Queen Aggravain has decreed no one can marry until her son, Prince Dauntless, finds a bride. Princesses line up to perform the ridiculous task of sensing a pea placed underneath twenty mattresses, but none can pass the test. Will the charming, free-spirited, and “shy” Winnifred the Woebegone become “the one”? At the 1960 Tony Awards, “Mattress” clinched a Best Musical nomination and a Best Actress nomination for then-newcomer Carol Burnett for her role as Winnifred.  

“As fresh, inventive and funny as ever... “ Once Upon a Mattress”?shows you can retell fairy tales with wit and sophistication, and more important, human feeling.” – New York Daily News  

Single tickets: $24 / $22 (Titan price)
All orders subject to a $4 service fee

All CSUF students may receive one (1) complimentary ticket to all music, theatre & dance performances.  More information

Book by Jay Thompson, Dean Fuller, and Marshall Barer
Directed by Josh Grisetti

Little Theatre

March 5 – 14, 2026

8pm: 3/5, 3/6, 3/7, 3/11, 3/12, 3/13, 3/14

2pm: 3/7, 3/14

“Once Upon a Mattress” is an uproarious update of Hans Christian Andersen’s “Princess and the Pea” set in a far-off land where Queen Aggravain has decreed no one can marry until her son, Prince Dauntless, finds a bride. Princesses line up to perform the ridiculous task of sensing a pea placed underneath twenty mattresses, but none can pass the test. Will the charming, free-spirited, and “shy” Winnifred the Woebegone become “the one”? At the 1960 Tony Awards, “Mattress” clinched a Best Musical nomination and a Best Actress nomination for then-newcomer Carol Burnett for her role as Winnifred.  

“As fresh, inventive and funny as ever... “ Once Upon a Mattress” shows you can retell fairy tales with wit and sophistication, and more important, human feeling.” – New York Daily News  

Single tickets: $24 / $22 (Titan price)
All orders subject to a $4 service fee

title: 19th Annual Collage Concert
description:
19th Annual Collage Concert

Saturday, March 14 at 3pm

Meng Concert Hall

 

Filled with inspiring performances and unforgettable musical moments, this annual event supports much-needed scholarship funds for School of Music students.

Revel in the sound as hundreds of student musicians perform on-stage, creating one continuous musical tapestry. From the sonorous voices of the University Singers to the swinging sounds of the Fullerton Jazz Orchestra, Collage represents the full breadth of the School of Music’s exceptional student ensembles and performers.

title: RHINOCEROS
description:
Rhinoceros

By Eugène Ionesco
Directed by Mark Ramont

March 19 – 28, 2026

Young Theatre

 

8pm: 3/19, 3/20, 3/21, 3/25, 3/26, 3/27, 3/28

2pm: 3/21, 3/28

The absurdist and allegorical “Rhinoceros” is a savage commentary on the human condition where a small French town is besieged by one roaring citizen who has transformed into a rhinoceros. While some are alarmed, others blithely ignore the intruder until one by one the citizenry, seduced by conformity and mob mentality, morph into a herd of rhinoceroses. But as the rhinos trample over the societal norms of an entire town, one man stubbornly refuses to change his form and identity. Who or what will prevail?

Single tickets: $14 / $12 (Titan price)
All orders subject to a $4 service fee

title: RHINOCEROS
description:
Rhinoceros

By Eugène Ionesco
Directed by Mark Ramont

March 19 – 28, 2026

Young Theatre

 

8pm: 3/19, 3/20, 3/21, 3/25, 3/26, 3/27, 3/28

2pm: 3/21, 3/28

The absurdist and allegorical “Rhinoceros” is a savage commentary on the human condition where a small French town is besieged by one roaring citizen who has transformed into a rhinoceros. While some are alarmed, others blithely ignore the intruder until one by one the citizenry, seduced by conformity and mob mentality, morph into a herd of rhinoceroses. But as the rhinos trample over the societal norms of an entire town, one man stubbornly refuses to change his form and identity. Who or what will prevail?

Single tickets: $14 / $12 (Titan price)
All orders subject to a $4 service fee

title: Karl Kramer-Johansen, french horn
description:
Karl Kramer-Johansen, french horn
with Dr. Alison Edwards, piano

Wednesday, April 8 at 8pm
Meng Concert Hall

Norwegian horn player Karl Kramer-Johansen is a much sought-after chamber musician, soloist, and recitalist. As a member of the Jupiter Symphony Chamber Players and the Dorian Wind Quintet, he performs regularly in New York City as well as touring across the country. A champion of new music, Kramer-Johansen is a member of Ensemble PI, and a guest with Parnassus, Sequitur and Argento.

title: X-Out
description:
X-Out

By Jack Novak
Directed by Miguel Torres Cruz

April 9-18, 2026

Hallberg Theatre

“X-out” is a dark, escalating farce centered on a group of high school cheerleaders who meet up for a weekend campout in the woods to perfect a dangerous stunt. But when a group of restless ghosts begin to possess them, their supernatural circumstances expose their deepest ambitions, fears, and secrets. “X-out” is a work of Devised Theatre that will undergo changes throughout the creative process.

title: 'Albert Herring' by Benjamin Britten
description:
'Albert Herring' by Benjamin Britten • Chamber Opera
Dr. Kerry Jennings & Kimo Furumoto, Directors


Thursday, April 16 - Sunday, April 19

8pm: April 16, 17, 18

3pm: April 19

Recital Hall

Regular and Titan price: $5

All orders subject to a $4 service fee

All CSUF students receive one (1) complimentary ticket to music, theatre & dance performances. 

More information

Benjamin Britten’s comic opera follows shy, virtuous Albert Herring as he rebels against his prudish upbringing after being crowned May King of his small English town. To celebrate, Albert goes on a wild pub crawl, challenging societal norms (and his mother's control). “Albert Herring” delivers themes of innocence, tradition, and rebellion with a sparkling score and sharp social commentary.

title: Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions
description:
Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 2, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

The landmark exhibition "Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions" celebrates the life, legacy, and enduring impact of Carole Caroompas (1946–2022) – a trailblazing Los Angeles-based artist and esteemed CSUF alumna.

Caroompas was widely recognized for her innovative approach to interweaving literature, pop culture, and feminist critique into layered visual narratives. Her work, developed over decades, continues to invite critical engagement among artists, scholars, and viewers for its rigorous interrogation of cultural norms and its complex, image-text constructions.

Carole Caroompas received her B.A. in English from CSU Fullerton in 1968, where early experiences in literature laid the groundwork for her interdisciplinary approach to artmaking. This foundation – further developed through her MFA studies in studio art at the University of Southern California—shaped a practice that merged literary inquiry with bold artistic experimentation. Caroompas’ ability to construct complex visual narratives positioned her as a transformative figure in conceptual and feminist art.

"Mystical Unions" revisits Caroompas’ deep ties to CSU Fullerton, where intellectual exploration and artistic risk-taking converged to shape her distinctive voice. Featuring key works from the university’s permanent collection alongside archival materials drawn from public and private sources, the exhibition highlights the dynamic interplay of text and image that defines her contribution to contemporary art and expands the narrative possibilities of painting in the late 20th and early 21st centuries.

ABOUT THE ARTIST

Carole Caroompas (1946–2022) was a visionary American painter whose work critically examined the intersections of pop culture, mythology, and gender archetypes. A graduate of California State University, Fullerton (B.A., 1968) and the University of Southern California (M.F.A., 1971), Caroompas developed a distinctive visual language that merged narrative structure with dense, symbolic imagery.

Her work has been exhibited widely in major institutions, including the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Modern Art in New York, the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA), the Hammer Museum at UCLA, and the Corcoran Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. Regionally, she held significant solo exhibitions at venues such as the Ben Maltz Gallery, Western Project, Mark Moore Gallery, P.P.O.W., and Sue Spaid Fine Art.

Throughout her career, Caroompas received numerous accolades, including a Guggenheim Fellowship, two National Endowment for the Arts grants in Painting, a COLA Individual Artist Fellowship from the City of Los Angeles, and a California Community Foundation Fellowship.

Presented in partnership with the Art Education Program at CSU Fullerton, Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions is curated by Jennifer Frias, Gallery Director/Curator, and Mary Anna Pomonis, Associate Professor of Art Education/Graduate Program Advisor, with research and written contributions by Elise Neal, Columbia College. Support for the exhibition is provided by the Art Alliance, the Instructional Related Activities Grant, the College of the Arts, and the Department of Visual Arts.

This exhibition is free and open to the public. For more information, including gallery hours and programming, visit  fullerton.edu/arts/galleries  or follow us on Instagram @cota_galleries_csuf.

IMAGE: CAROLE CAROOMPAS, Hester and Zoro: In Quest of a New World – The Little Book that Carries Woes, 1985. Acrylic on canvas, 48 x 84 inches. CSUF Department of Visual Arts Permanent Collection. Gift of Cliff Benjamin. [2023.000611]

title: Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)
description:
Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 16, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

Known for her meticulous process of cutting and layering photographs, Soo Kim’s latest exhibition challenges traditional expectations of the medium as a form of documentation. In this new body of work, photography operates as a site of material and conceptual exploration, encompassing hand-cut prints, paper compositions, immersive slide projections, and collected ephemera. The works reflect ongoing themes in Kim’s practice—impermanence, fragmentation, and the interplay between visibility and erasure.

Also on view are materials drawn from the artist’s studio—literary excerpts, handwritten notes, found objects, and other personal references that have informed the work’s development. These items appear alongside the artworks, offering a broader context for understanding Kim’s creative process and the visual, textual, and emotional references that shape it.

title: Spring Student Exhibitions | Group 2
description:
College of the Arts Galleries

SPRING STUDENT EXHIBITIONS | GROUP 2
Public Reception | March 4 from 4–6 PM
Exhibitions on view March 3–7

Student Exhibition Calendar

PAUL GARCIA
Creative Photography & Experimental Media, BFA
The Cost of Peace
Duff Gallery

The Cost of Peace is an examination of the costs of achieving tranquility and how stability always leaves its traces behind.

DÉJÀ VU
Glass, BFA
Good Morning, Good Knight
Freedman Gallery

Vu’s work explores self-acceptance and highlights inner beauty through the subversion of appearances and expectations.


LESLIE PEDROZA
Teaching, BA
Y Me Vale

"Healing and reclaiming the discarded parts of myself, my childhood and my Mexican heritage, that were ignored or made small."

title: Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)
description:
Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 16, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

Known for her meticulous process of cutting and layering photographs, Soo Kim’s latest exhibition challenges traditional expectations of the medium as a form of documentation. In this new body of work, photography operates as a site of material and conceptual exploration, encompassing hand-cut prints, paper compositions, immersive slide projections, and collected ephemera. The works reflect ongoing themes in Kim’s practice—impermanence, fragmentation, and the interplay between visibility and erasure.

Also on view are materials drawn from the artist’s studio—literary excerpts, handwritten notes, found objects, and other personal references that have informed the work’s development. These items appear alongside the artworks, offering a broader context for understanding Kim’s creative process and the visual, textual, and emotional references that shape it.

The exhibition is organized by CSUF College of the Arts Galleries and is curated by Jennifer Frias, Gallery Director/Curator and Jennifer Lee, Curatorial Assistant (Art History, ’25). Support for the exhibition is provided by the Art Alliance, the Instructional Related Activities Grant, the College of the Arts, and the Department of Visual Arts. 

ABOUT THE ARTIST

Soo Kim is a photo-based artist whose work has been exhibited at institutions including the Getty Center, the Gwangju Biennale, the Pasadena Museum of California Art, the Orange County Museum of Art, and the Seoul Museum of Art. Her work is held in the collections of the J. Paul Getty Museum, The Broad Foundation, LACMA, and the Albright-Knox Art Gallery, among others. She received her MFA from CalArts and currently serves as Professor and Program Director of Photography at Otis College of Art and Design, where she also leads the Critic-in-Residence program.

This exhibition is free and open to the public. For more information, including gallery hours and programming, visit  fullerton.edu/arts/galleries  or follow us on Instagram @cota_galleries_csuf.

COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

Known for her meticulous process of cutting and layering photographs, Soo Kim’s latest exhibition challenges traditional expectations of the medium as a form of documentation. The works reflect ongoing themes in Kim’s practice—impermanence, fragmentation, and the interplay between visibility and erasure.

title: Spring Student Exhibitions | Group 3
description:
College of the Arts Galleries

SPRING STUDENT EXHIBITIONS | GROUP 2
Public Reception | March 18 from 4–6 PM
Exhibitions on view March 17–21

Student Exhibition Calendar

JONATHAN HERNANDEZ
Drawing & Painting, BFA
Looking Forward to Tomorrow
Duff Gallery

The exhibition explores the tension of living through a time when simple moments are no longer guaranteed.

COLTON ALLEN
Sculpture, BFA
Worlds of Wonder

In Worlds of Wonder, Allen will create a world that evokes childlike wonder where every detail is considered and intentionally incorporated.

DANIELA NAVARRO
Creative Photography & Experimental Media, BFA
Through the Lens of Care

Through her work, Navarro creates space for caregiving as complex and real, marked by love, strain, routine, and presence.

title: Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)
description:
Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 16, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

Known for her meticulous process of cutting and layering photographs, Soo Kim’s latest exhibition challenges traditional expectations of the medium as a form of documentation. In this new body of work, photography operates as a site of material and conceptual exploration, encompassing hand-cut prints, paper compositions, immersive slide projections, and collected ephemera. The works reflect ongoing themes in Kim’s practice—impermanence, fragmentation, and the interplay between visibility and erasure.

Also on view are materials drawn from the artist’s studio—literary excerpts, handwritten notes, found objects, and other personal references that have informed the work’s development. These items appear alongside the artworks, offering a broader context for understanding Kim’s creative process and the visual, textual, and emotional references that shape it.

The exhibition is organized by CSUF College of the Arts Galleries and is curated by Jennifer Frias, Gallery Director/Curator and Jennifer Lee, Curatorial Assistant (Art History, ’25). Support for the exhibition is provided by the Art Alliance, the Instructional Related Activities Grant, the College of the Arts, and the Department of Visual Arts.

Her work is held in the collections of the J. Paul Getty Museum, The Broad Foundation, LACMA, and the Albright-Knox Art Gallery, among others. She received her MFA from CalArts and currently serves as Professor and Program Director of Photography at Otis College of Art and Design, where she also leads the Critic-in-Residence program.

This exhibition is free and open to the public. For more information, including gallery hours and programming, visit  fullerton.edu/arts/galleries  or follow us on Instagram @cota_galleries_csuf.

title: Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)
description:
Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 16, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

Known for her meticulous process of cutting and layering photographs, Soo Kim’s latest exhibition challenges traditional expectations of the medium as a form of documentation. In this new body of work, photography operates as a site of material and conceptual exploration, encompassing hand-cut prints, paper compositions, immersive slide projections, and collected ephemera. The works reflect ongoing themes in Kim’s practice—impermanence, fragmentation, and the interplay between visibility and erasure.

Also on view are materials drawn from the artist’s studio—literary excerpts, handwritten notes, found objects, and other personal references that have informed the work’s development. These items appear alongside the artworks, offering a broader context for understanding Kim’s creative process and the visual, textual, and emotional references that shape it.

The exhibition is organized by CSUF College of the Arts Galleries and is curated by Jennifer Frias, Gallery Director/Curator and Jennifer Lee, Curatorial Assistant (Art History, ’25). Support for the exhibition is provided by the Art Alliance, the Instructional Related Activities Grant, the College of the Arts, and the Department of Visual Arts.

title: Spring Student Exhibitions | Group 4
description:
College of the Arts Galleries

SPRING STUDENT EXHIBITIONS | GROUP 4
Public Reception | April 15 from 4–6 PM
Exhibitions on view April 14–18

Student Exhibition Calendar

ADELINE WONG
Graphic and Interactive Design, BFA
On a Visa
Duff Gallery

A visual representation of life as an international student in the current political climate, fighting and hoping to stay whilst still reminiscing about the home country.

JOSHUA EADS
General Studio Art, BA
POP…Propaganda
Freedman Gallery

Eads uses a visual language he calls "Neo-Propaganda Pop" to look at how persuasive imagery works and why it’s so powerful in contemporary culture.


KAT CARCOBA
Sculpture, MFA
Eternidad

Kathleen Carcoba is a multidisciplinary artist working across sculptural and spatial forms to explore memory, identity, and material.

SPRING STUDENT EXHIBITIONS | GROUP 4
Public Reception | April 15 from 4–6 PM
Exhibitions on view April 14–18

Student Exhibition Calendar

ADELINE WONG
Graphic and Interactive Design, BFA
On a Visa
Duff Gallery

A visual representation of life as an international student in the current political climate, fighting and hoping to stay whilst still reminiscing about the home country.

JOSHUA EADS
General Studio Art, BA
POP…Propaganda
Freedman Gallery

Eads uses a visual language he calls "Neo-Propaganda Pop" to look at how persuasive imagery works and why it’s so powerful in contemporary culture.


KAT CARCOBA
Sculpture, MFA
Eternidad

Kathleen Carcoba is a multidisciplinary artist working across sculptural and spatial forms to explore memory, identity, and material.

title: Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions
description:
Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 2, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

The landmark exhibition "Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions" celebrates the life, legacy, and enduring impact of Carole Caroompas (1946–2022) – a trailblazing Los Angeles-based artist and esteemed CSUF alumna.

Caroompas was widely recognized for her innovative approach to interweaving literature, pop culture, and feminist critique into layered visual narratives. Her work, developed over decades, continues to invite critical engagement among artists, scholars, and viewers for its rigorous interrogation of cultural norms and its complex, image-text constructions.

Carole Caroompas received her B.A. in English from CSU Fullerton in 1968, where early experiences in literature laid the groundwork for her interdisciplinary approach to artmaking. This foundation – further developed through her MFA studies in studio art at the University of Southern California—shaped a practice that merged literary inquiry with bold artistic experimentation. Caroompas’ ability to construct complex visual narratives positioned her as a transformative figure in conceptual and feminist art.

"Mystical Unions" revisits Caroompas’ deep ties to CSU Fullerton, where intellectual exploration and artistic risk-taking converged to shape her distinctive voice. Featuring key works from the university’s permanent collection alongside archival materials drawn from public and private sources, the exhibition highlights the dynamic interplay of text and image that defines her contribution to contemporary art and expands the narrative possibilities of painting in the late 20th and early 21st centuries.

ABOUT THE ARTIST

Carole Caroompas (1946–2022) was a visionary American painter whose work critically examined the intersections of pop culture, mythology, and gender archetypes. A graduate of California State University, Fullerton (B.A., 1968) and the University of Southern California (M.F.A., 1971), Caroompas developed a distinctive visual language that merged narrative structure with dense, symbolic imagery.

Her work has been exhibited widely in major institutions, including the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Modern Art in New York, the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA), the Hammer Museum at UCLA, and the Corcoran Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. Regionally, she held significant solo exhibitions at venues such as the Ben Maltz Gallery, Western Project, Mark Moore Gallery, P.P.O.W., and Sue Spaid Fine Art.

Throughout her career, Caroompas received numerous accolades, including a Guggenheim Fellowship, two National Endowment for the Arts grants in Painting, a COLA Individual Artist Fellowship from the City of Los Angeles, and a California Community Foundation Fellowship.

Presented in partnership with the Art Education Program at CSU Fullerton, Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions is curated by Jennifer Frias, Gallery Director/Curator, and Mary Anna Pomonis, Associate Professor of Art Education/Graduate Program Advisor, with research and written contributions by Elise Neal, Columbia College. Support for the exhibition is provided by the Art Alliance, the Instructional Related Activities Grant, the College of the Arts, and the Department of Visual Arts.

This exhibition is free and open to the public. For more information, including gallery hours and programming, visit  fullerton.edu/arts/galleries  or follow us on Instagram @cota_galleries_csuf.

IMAGE: CAROLE CAROOMPAS, Hester and Zoro: In Quest of a New World – The Little Book that Carries Woes, 1985. Acrylic on canvas, 48 x 84 inches. CSUF Department of Visual Arts Permanent Collection. Gift of Cliff Benjamin. [2023.000611]

title: Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)
description:
Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 16, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

Known for her meticulous process of cutting and layering photographs, Soo Kim’s latest exhibition challenges traditional expectations of the medium as a form of documentation. In this new body of work, photography operates as a site of material and conceptual exploration, encompassing hand-cut prints, paper compositions, immersive slide projections, and collected ephemera. The works reflect ongoing themes in Kim’s practice—impermanence, fragmentation, and the interplay between visibility and erasure.

Also on view are materials drawn from the artist’s studio—literary excerpts, handwritten notes, found objects, and other personal references that have informed the work’s development. These items appear alongside the artworks, offering a broader context for understanding Kim’s creative process and the visual, textual, and emotional references that shape it.

The exhibition is organized by CSUF College of the Arts Galleries and is curated by Jennifer Frias, Gallery Director/Curator and Jennifer Lee, Curatorial Assistant (Art History, ’25). Support for the exhibition is provided by the Art Alliance, the Instructional Related Activities Grant, the College of the Arts, and the Department of Visual Arts. 

ABOUT THE ARTIST

Soo Kim is a photo-based artist whose work has been exhibited at institutions including the Getty Center, the Gwangju Biennale, the Pasadena Museum of California Art, the Orange County Museum of Art, and the Seoul Museum of Art. Her work is held in the collections of the J. Paul Getty Museum, The Broad Foundation, LACMA, and the Albright-Knox Art Gallery, among others. She received her MFA from CalArts and currently serves as Professor and Program Director of Photography at Otis College of Art and Design, where she also leads the Critic-in-Residence program.

This exhibition is free and open to the public. For more information, including gallery hours and programming, visit  fullerton.edu/arts/galleries  or follow us on Instagram @cota_galleries_csuf.

RELATED PROGRAMMING

(To be announced)


IMAGE: SOO KIM, Passage - (She goes to the window. The bell music briefly sounds. The woman from across the way enters), 2025. Hand-cut archival pigment print, acrylic lacquer. Courtesy of the artist.

title: Spring Student Exhibitions | Group 5
description:
College of the Arts Galleries

SPRING STUDENT EXHIBITIONS | GROUP 5
Public Reception | April 15 from 4–6 PM
Exhibitions on view April 14–18

Student Exhibition Calendar

MELISSA KIM
Illustration, MFA
Paws and Drinks
Duff Gallery

This series explores companionship through the world of bartending. Together, these works honor ritual, presence, and the comfort of being quietly witnessed.

HAILEY CISNEROS
Drawing & Painting, BFA
Year of the Horse
Freedman Gallery

Cisneros found inspiration for her Freedman Gallery show by painting the subject of her childhood wonder: Horses.


CINDY MARTINEZ
General Studio Art, BA
Interruptions of Perfection

The artist explores the captivating intersection of nature and imagination, where bold landscapes invite viewer into a world of curiosity.

COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

The landmark exhibition "Carole Caroompas: Mystical Unions" celebrates the life, legacy, and enduring impact of Carole Caroompas (1946–2022) – a trailblazing Los Angeles-based artist and esteemed CSUF alumna.

Caroompas was widely recognized for her innovative approach to interweaving literature, pop culture, and feminist critique into layered visual narratives. Her work, developed over decades, continues to invite critical engagement among artists, scholars, and viewers for its rigorous interrogation of cultural norms and its complex, image-text constructions.

Carole Caroompas received her B.A. in English from CSU Fullerton in 1968, where early experiences in literature laid the groundwork for her interdisciplinary approach to artmaking. This foundation – further developed through her MFA studies in studio art at the University of Southern California—shaped a practice that merged literary inquiry with bold artistic experimentation. Caroompas’ ability to construct complex visual narratives positioned her as a transformative figure in conceptual and feminist art.

"Mystical Unions" revisits Caroompas’ deep ties to CSU Fullerton, where intellectual exploration and artistic risk-taking converged to shape her distinctive voice. Featuring key works from the university’s permanent collection alongside archival materials drawn from public and private sources, the exhibition highlights the dynamic interplay of text and image that defines her contribution to contemporary art and expands the narrative possibilities of painting in the late 20th and early 21st centuries.

ABOUT THE ARTIST

Carole Caroompas (1946–2022) was a visionary American painter whose work critically examined the intersections of pop culture, mythology, and gender archetypes. A graduate of California State University, Fullerton (B.A., 1968) and the University of Southern California (M.F.A., 1971), Caroompas developed a distinctive visual language that merged narrative structure with dense, symbolic imagery.

Her work has been exhibited widely in major institutions, including the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Modern Art in New York, the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA), the Hammer Museum at UCLA, and the Corcoran Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. title: Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)
description:
Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 16, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

Known for her meticulous process of cutting and layering photographs, Soo Kim’s latest exhibition challenges traditional expectations of the medium as a form of documentation. In this new body of work, photography operates as a site of material and conceptual exploration, encompassing hand-cut prints, paper compositions, immersive slide projections, and collected ephemera. The works reflect ongoing themes in Kim’s practice—impermanence, fragmentation, and the interplay between visibility and erasure.

Also on view are materials drawn from the artist’s studio—literary excerpts, handwritten notes, found objects, and other personal references that have informed the work’s development. These items appear alongside the artworks, offering a broader context for understanding Kim’s creative process and the visual, textual, and emotional references that shape it.

The exhibition is organized by CSUF College of the Arts Galleries and is curated by Jennifer Frias, Gallery Director/Curator and Jennifer Lee, Curatorial Assistant (Art History, ’25). Support for the exhibition is provided by the Art Alliance, the Instructional Related Activities Grant, the College of the Arts, and the Department of Visual Arts.

title: Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)
description:
Soo Kim: (Charlie sings in the quietest voice)



College of the Arts Galleries | Visual Arts Complex, Building G

Exhibition Dates: October 4, 2025 – May 16, 2026
Location: Begovich Gallery 
Gallery Hours + Visitor's Information
COTA Galleries Artist Lecture Series + InConversation

Known for her meticulous process of cutting and layering photographs, Soo Kim’s latest exhibition challenges traditional expectations of the medium as a form of documentation. In this new body of work, photography operates as a site of material and conceptual exploration, encompassing hand-cut prints, paper compositions, immersive slide projections, and collected ephemera. The works reflect ongoing themes in Kim’s practice—impermanence, fragmentation, and the interplay between visibility and erasure.

Also on view are materials drawn from the artist’s studio—literary excerpts, handwritten notes, found objects, and other personal references that have informed the work’s development. These items appear alongside the artworks, offering a broader context for understanding Kim’s creative process and the visual, textual, and emotional references that shape it.

The exhibition is organized by CSUF College of the Arts Galleries and is curated by Jennifer Frias, Gallery Director/Curator and Jennifer Lee, Curatorial Assistant (Art History, ’25). Support for the exhibition is provided by the Art Alliance, the Instructional Related Activities Grant, the College of the Arts, and the Department of Visual Arts. 

ABOUT THE ARTIST

Soo Kim is a photo-based artist whose work has been exhibited at institutions including the Getty Center, the Gwangju Biennale, the Pasadena Museum of California Art, the Orange County Museum of Art, and the Seoul Museum of Art. Her work is held in the collections of the J. Paul Getty Museum, The Broad Foundation, LACMA, and the Albright-Knox Art Gallery, among others. She received her MFA from CalArts and currently serves as Professor and Program Director of Photography at Otis College of Art and Design, where she also leads the Critic-in-Residence program.

This exhibition is free and open to the public. For more information, including gallery hours and programming, visit  fullerton.edu/arts/galleries  or follow us on Instagram @cota_galleries_csuf.

title: Applying to Scholarships at CSUF Webinar
description: Join the Scholarships Office via Zoom for a virtual presentation on what to expect when applying for CSUF Scholarships, Outside Scholarships, and other funding opportunities. A live message chat will be available during and after the session for students and families to ask questions.

title: FDC: NeuroPREP (Asynchronous)
description:
FDC: NeuroPREP (Asynchronous)
02/17/26 - 03/13/26
(Asynchronous)


Neurodiversity Prep, or NeuroPREP, is a faculty training program that offers information on neurodiversity, autism, and ADHD to faculty in higher education to understand the postsecondary experiences of neurodivergent students. NeuroPREP focuses on relevant knowledge, applied strategies, and accessible resources for improving postsecondary experiences and outcomes for these students. Further, NeuroPREP facilitates an understanding of best practices, campus supports, and higher education federal law so that faculty are equipped to support neurodivergent students in the classroom. Faculty with varying levels of teaching experience and experience working with neurodivergent students are welcome to participate! As a NeuroPREP participant, your involvement includes the completion of four self-paced online video modules and accompanying module worksheets (~2 hours to complete; this asynchronous course includes Parts 1 and 2 of the synchronous version).

Register for 'NeuroPREP (Asynchronous)'

title: FDC: Introduction to Structural Equation Modeling
description:
FDC: Introduction to Structural Equation Modeling
02/23/26 - 02/27/26
(Asynchronous)

This workshop will focus on running and reporting basic structural equation models (SEM).  We will begin with a conceptual overview of SEM focusing on the Bentler-Weeks formulation.  Presuming the data have met the necessary assumptions (reviewed briefly), we will cover how to specify a basic SEM for analysis using the Diagrammer in EQS to write nearly all the necessary EQS syntax to run an SEM model.  We will examine the output and discuss “what to report” (e.g., adequacy of the measurement model, recommended fit indices, etc.), examine model modification indices, and present effect decomposition.  Finally, we will translate the model developed in the EQS Diagrammer, along with the EQS output, into a manuscript-ready model using LaTeX software.

Register for 'Introduction to Structural Equation Modeling'

title: FDC: Exploring your data in Nvivo
description:
FDC: Exploring your data in Nvivo
02/25/26
1:00pm - 2:00pm (Zoom)

This workshop is designed to be a follow-up to the introduction to Nvivo workshop. Participants will learn the basic analytical tools in Nvivo including running queries, exploring multiple patterns in the data, creating thinking maps and mergining projects.

Register for 'Exploring your data in Nvivo'

title: FDC: Learn About Your Grad Students – A Data-Driven Guide for Better Teaching
description:
FDC: Learn About Your Grad Students – A Data-Driven Guide for Better Teaching
02/26/26
1:00 pm - 2:00 pm (Zoom)

Our campus serves almost 5000 graduate students, but our campus-wide conversations rarely focus on their stories and background. As a result, faculty often make assumptions based on our undergraduates, or on our own graduate school experiences. Neither are good guides. In this practical workshop, the former program director for Project upGRADS and ELEVAR explores a data-based approach to a better understanding of our program’s graduate cohorts.

Register for 'Learn About Your Grad Students – A Data-Driven Guide for Better Teaching'

Register for 'Introduction to Excel'

title: FDC: Neurodiversity 101 (Asynchronous)
description:
FDC: Neurodiversity 101 (Asynchronous)
03/02/26 - 03/13/26
(Asynchronous)

“Neurodiversity” is a term and movement that refers to diversity, or normal variation, of the human mind. There are many different definitions and interpretations of the term, as well as various diagnostic labels that are represented by it. This one-hour workshop will introduce participants to the terms and definitions commonly associated with “neurodiversity” and provide resources for diving further into the topic.

Register for 'Neurodiversity 101 (Asynchronous)'

title: FDC: Mentoring Grad Students Through Precarity
description:
FDC: Mentoring Grad Students Through Precarity
03/04/26
10:00 am - 11:00 am (Zoom)

This discussion-oriented workshop focuses on the precarious worlds of Cal State Fullerton graduate students and faculty at various ranks. Starting from a standpoint of acknowledgment — precarity exists and is not going away anytime soon — we will tackle what this means for us and the students we advise, mentor, and teach. After exploring various experiences of precarity, we will work through several case studies and discuss practical strategies to mitigate the impacts of precarity while strategizing ways to foster work-life harmony and degree completion.

Register for 'Mentoring Grad Students Through Precarity'

Zoom link: https://fullerton.zoom.us/j/87089514233

title: FDC: The Resilient Faculty - Thriving Without the Superhero Cape
description:
FDC: The Resilient Faculty - Thriving Without the Superhero Cape
03/05/26
1:00 pm - 2:00 pm (Zoom)

Are you wearing the superhero cape but running on empty? This workshop is designed to help faculty prioritize their own well-being while continuing to support their students effectively. Explore practical self-care strategies and learn how to build healthy systems, routines, and boundaries with graduate students. Leave with actionable tools and fresh ideas to foster resilience and sustainability in your academic life.

Register for 'The Resilient Faculty: Thriving Without the Superhero Cape'

Zoom link: https://fullerton.zoom.us/j/87017009505

title: FDC: Intermediate Excel
description:
FDC: Intermediate Excel
03/09/26 - 03/13/26
(Asynchronous)

Description: This workshop is designed for faculty who have used Excel before and want to expand their knowledge.  Improving formatting, organizing data, highlighting key information, and creating formulas/links between sheets.  Specifically, we will be using Excel to quickly summarize multiple sheets of data into one, turn long lists and reports into easy to read tables, use formulas to check whether cells pass or fail specified rules, create links between cells so that they update automatically, and highlight targets, trends, duplicates and errors with Conditional Formatting.

Register for 'Intermediate Excel'

title: FDC: Gentle Decluttering: A Mindful Approach to Letting Go
description:
FDC: Gentle Decluttering: A Mindful Approach to Letting Go
03/10/26
1:30 pm - 2:30 pm (Hybrid)


Carrie Lane, Chair and Professor of American Studies, spent a decade interviewing and working alongside professional organizers. In this workshop, Dr. Lane shares her favorite tips for decluttering our homes and workspaces while discussing why we have so much stuff in the first place. (Nope, it's not just that we buy too much.)

Register for 'Gentle Decluttering: A Mindful Approach to Letting Go'

03/16/26 - 03/20/26

title: FDC: Feeling Seen and Heard: Using Communicative Immediacy to Build Trust Across Difference
description:
FDC: Feeling Seen and Heard: Using Communicative Immediacy to Build Trust Across Difference
03/17/26
11:00 am - 12:00 pm (Zoom)

This workshop explores how immediacy behaviors, e.g. verbal strategies, accessibility cues, and forms of nonverbal engagement, can counteract uncertainty, distancing, and difference and, instead, foster an environment where mentees of diverse backgrounds feel empowered and connected. Participants will examine and consider how interpersonal and intercultural communication tools can foster trust, navigate misunderstandings, and result in growth and support for mentees (and mentors).

Register for 'Feeling Seen and Heard: Using Communicative Immediacy to Build Trust Across Difference'

title: FDC: Creating Clear and Useful Rubrics for Student Assessment in the College Classroom
description:
FDC: Creating Clear and Useful Rubrics for Student Assessment in the College Classroom
03/25/26
12:30 pm - 1:45 pm
PLS-256

A rubric is a powerful tool for assessing student performance in your classroom. But it can also be used to focus and guide student progress toward mastery. Students who can clearly understand educational outcomes are more likely to work toward and achieve those outcomes with focus and clarity. This session will examine how to work backwards from your specific educational course objectives to create clear rubrics that describe what students will do to achieve a certain grade, and we will create several rubrics during the session that you can use in your classes.

Register for 'Creating Clear and Useful Rubrics for Student Assessment in the College Classroom'

title: FDC: Introduction to Item Response Theory
description:
FDC: Introduction to Item Response Theory
04/06/26 - 04/10/26
(Asynchronous)

An essential aspect of psychological research is the measurement of individuals on a construct of interest.  Furthermore, accurate measurement of individuals is imperative when high-stakes decisions are involved.  Therefore, much of psychological research comprises the development, revision, and application of measurement instruments.  The focus of this workshop will be on applications of Item Response Theory (IRT) models to measuring psychological phenomenon. The workshop will begin with a brief discussion/review of related topics (e.g., classical test theory, logistic regression).  After establishing IRT fundamentals, the assumptions and modeling techniques of IRT models for dichotomous data will be illustrated conceptually using modern statistical software, R.  Finally, utilization of various dichotomous IRT models to inform scale revision will be demonstrated and applied to example datasets.  Basic knowledge of R is recommended, but not required.

Register for 'Introduction to Item Response Theory'

title: FDC: NeuroPREP (Asynchronous)
description:
FDC: NeuroPREP (Asynchronous)
04/06/26 - 05/01/26
(Asynchronous)

Neurodiversity Prep, or NeuroPREP, is a faculty training program that offers information on neurodiversity, autism, and ADHD to faculty in higher education to understand the postsecondary experiences of neurodivergent students. NeuroPREP focuses on relevant knowledge, applied strategies, and accessible resources for improving postsecondary experiences and outcomes for these students. Further, NeuroPREP facilitates an understanding of best practices, campus supports, and higher education federal law so that faculty are equipped to support neurodivergent students in the classroom. Faculty with varying levels of teaching experience and experience working with neurodivergent students are welcome to participate! As a NeuroPREP participant, your involvement includes the completion of four self-paced online video modules and accompanying module worksheets (~2 hours to complete; this asynchronous course includes Parts 1 and 2 of the synchronous version).

Register for 'NeuroPREP (Asynchronous)'

title: FDC: Creating Meaningful Discussions in Your Classroom
description:
FDC: Creating Meaningful Discussions in Your Classroom
04/15/26
12:30 pm - 1:45 pm
PLS-256

It has been posited that discussion, as a classroom teaching technique, is the most difficult pedagogical teaching tool to administer well. Research has shown that teachers can mistake quizzing and classroom conversation for true "discussion." This seminar will examine discussion as a teaching tool, clarify what should not qualify as functional classroom discussion, and examine how the questions we use in our classroom can greatly affect how students think, react, and participate in our college classrooms.

Register for 'Creating Meaningful Discussions in Your Classroom'

FDC: Neurodiversity Conversations: Supporting Faculty in the Classroom
04/17/26
11:00 am - 12:00 pm (Zoom)

Neurodiversity Conversations: Supporting Faculty in the Classroom is a discussion-based session designed for faculty and staff interested in strengthening inclusive and accessible teaching practices. Participants will engage in open-ended conversations and Q&A around topics such as universal design for learning, neuroinclusive pedagogy, student self-advocacy, and strategies for fostering equitable learning environments. Whether you’re exploring new approaches to course design, navigating questions about classroom dynamics, or seeking concrete tools to support neurodivergent learners and yourself, this session aims to spark collaboration, reflection, and actionable ideas for more inclusive teaching.

title: SDC Boost: "The Best Career Path Isn't Always a Straight Line"
description:
"The Best Career Path Isn't Always a Straight Line"

Conventional wisdom frames the ideal career path as a linear one -- a ladder to be climbed with a single-minded focus to get to the top. Career development consultants Sarah Ellis and Helen Tupper invite you to replace this outdated and limiting model with "squiggly" careers: dynamic, open-ended growth paths tailor-made for your individual needs, talents and ambitions. A radical rethink for anyone who feels restricted and defined by the limits of the corporate ladder.

May 13, 2026

12:00pm - 1:15pm

title: Academic Internship Information Session - General
description:
The Center for Internships & Community Engagement (CICE) offers this session for Juniors and Seniors who will be, or are interested in, taking an academic internship course. Join us to learn about the resources available and processes required to search for and secure an academic internship opportunity. We'll cover the steps required to receive CICE approval and complete the CICE Placement process. Before attending, check the Academic Internship Instructions for your course to learn more details and ensure applicability for your major/minor. No RSVP required.

Join via Zoom

Note the following disciplines offer specific information sessions for academic internship courses which you are required to attend in place of this session:


  • Child and Adolescent Studies
  • Cinema & Television Arts
  • Communications
  • Human Communications
  • Human Services
  • Psychology

Please visit the CICE Academic Internship Student page for listings of all upcoming information sessions.

Contact CICE on (657) 278-3746 with questions about this or any of our academic internship or practicum information sessions.

title: 2026-27 FAFSA & CADAA Zoom Drop-In
location: Titan Hub, Division of Student Affairs, Office of Financial Aid
description:
Location: Zoom Room

Click here to Register:

Join the Titan Hub and the Office of Financial Aid during our virtual drop in event and ask questions one-on-one with a financial aid expert to help you apply for financial aid using the 2026-27 FAFSA or California Dream Act Application (CADAA).   

We recommend having the following ready:   

- If possible, use a desktop/laptop (vs a smartphone), this will make it easier to share your screen and fill out your FAFSA/CADAA 

- Social Security Card or Number or green card (for FAFSA) 

- Have your (and your contributors, if applicable) 2024 tax documents ready (ex. federal tax forms, W-2, or forms of pay records) AND any Records of child support received (if applicable) AND Current balances of cash, savings, and checking accounts

- If submitting a FAFSA, have both yours and your contributors FSA ID username and password ready (create one if you haven't already done so)

- You can view Tips for Preparing to Fill out the FAFSA Form

title: 2026-27 CADAA/FAFSA Pop Up
location: Titan Hub, Division of Student Affairs, Office of Financial Aid
description:
Location: Titan Walk Table 10

- If possible, use a desktop/laptop (vs a smartphone), this will make it easier to share your screen and fill out your FAFSA/CADAA 

- Social Security Card or Number or green card (for FAFSA) 

- Have your (and your contributors, if applicable) 2024 tax documents ready (ex. federal tax forms, W-2, or forms of pay records) AND any Records of child support received (if applicable) AND Current balances of cash, savings, and checking accounts

- If submitting a FAFSA, have both yours and your contributors FSA ID username and password ready (create one if you haven't already done so)

- You can view Tips for Preparing to Fill out the FAFSA Form

title: TitanMED Mobile Health Clinic
location: TitanHEALTH, Division of Student Affairs, TitanMED, TitanTHRIVE, TitanWELL
description:
We’re bringing health and wellness directly to you!

Mobile Health Clinics are coming to campus to provide FREE services for all students, including:

 


  • Flu Shots
  • HIV Screening
  • Recovery Boots for Muscle Recovery
  • Naloxone Training (Opioid Overdose Prevention) – includes a FREE naloxone kit upon completion
  • RAP Cart

 

No appointment needed – just stop by, take care of your health, and walk away with free resources and support. Your health matters. Let us help you stay strong, safe, and informed this semester.

title: CHOMP: Cook & Connect
location: TitanWELL, Division of Student Affairs, TitanHEALTH, TitanMED, TitanTHRIVE
description:
One-Pot Wonders: Flavor Without the Fuss - Soups and Stews (Latin & Asian flavor profiles)

Learn simple tips for preparing delicious, affordable meals while enjoying a relaxed, social atmosphere. We’ll provide all the ingredients and guidance. Free food & giveaways!

Central Quad

location: Division of Student Affairs, Student Wellness, TitanHEALTH, TitanWELL
description:
The RAP Cart is a roaming safe sex supplies resource that brings free condoms, lubricants, and educational materials directly to the Titan community. Staffed by trained peer educators, it promotes safer sex practices and provides judgment-free access to essential sexual health supplies across campus.

Location: Library

title: Sustainability Walking Tour
location: University Sustainability, Capital Programs & Facilities Management
description:
Join University Sustainability and Capital Programs & Facilities Management for a walk around campus to see our commitment to a greener future in action! Please wear comfortable closed-toe walking shoes. The tour will last approximately 2 hours.

Please RSVP by filling out this form: Sustainability Walking Tours RSVP

This tour will highlight key sustainability features:


  • Green buildings
  • Solar PV and Energy Systems
  • Water conservation efforts
  • Landscape Management
  • Transportation initiatives
  • Waste Reduction programs
  • Campus Garden

Date: Thursday, March 12, 2026

Time: 10:00 AM - 12:00 PM

Location: Becker Amphitheater

title: Grant: Faculty and Student of the Year Nomination App Closes
description:
Mentoring is life-changing. It is a fundamental determinant of success in all fields and the primary driver of UROC’s mission. These awards recognize outstanding faculty mentors and student mentees demonstrating a longstanding commitment to extracurricular research, and scholarly and creative activities. The UROC Mentor and Mentee Awards honor those who contribute to an engaging, supportive, inclusive academic environment through their faculty mentorship of students or student participation.

Award categories include:


  • Faculty Mentoring Undergraduate and/or Graduate Students
  • Undergraduate Student of the Year
  • Graduate Student of the Year

Please nominate students or faculty using this link by March 2nd, 2026.

title: UROC Undergraduate and Graduate Travel Grants
description: The Undergraduate Research Opportunity Center — UROC — announces a new funding opportunity aimed to support undergraduate and graduate student travel: UROC Student Travel Grant funds travel and conference registration expenses for ALL CSUF students participating in professional conferences, performances, art exhibitions, and other professional activities wherein the student is activity participating in research or creative projects or performing activities. Students apply online via Info Ready~ by the advertised due date. Applications will be reviewed on a rolling basis and awards range between $200-$750.

title: NCUR Registration Closes
description:
Registration for the National Conference on Undergraduate Research (NCUR) begins on January 14th, 2026, and ends on March 6th, 2026. 

The conference will take place between April 13-15, 2026 in Richmond. If you are interested in presenting your research, NCUR is a great opportunity to gain experience and meet other young researchers. 

If you are interested in applying for a UROC Travel Grant, please apply here by February 1st.

title: Grant: BB2P CSUF 2026-2027 Track 2 Application Closes
description:
Are you already doing research? Want to get more out of it?

Building Bridges to the Professoriate (BB2P) is a collaborative program involving UC Riverside, Cal State Fresno, and Cal State Fullerton, supported by the UC-HSI Doctoral Diversity Initiative (UC-HSI-DDI).

Eligibility: Undergraduate or Master's students currently conducting research with a CSUF professor who are interested in pursuing a PhD.

BB2P Scholars participate in professional development activities, and funding supports travel of scholars to BB2P events & their research at CSUF.

This application is due on March 27th, 2026.
